Lecture 9: Constructing Projections ​

Topics: §3.5 — Uniqueness of Projections, Construction Formula Date: Apr 20–23, 2026

🎯 Overview ​

In previous lectures, we learned:

  • Lecture 7: Definition of projection (P2=P), geometric model, interchanging property (Col(P)=Null(I−P))
  • Lecture 8: Decomposition properties, rank(P)=trace(P), compatible families

Today, we solve the construction problem: Given two subspaces W and S, how do we construct a projection P with Col(P)=W and Null(P)=S?

Key questions:

  1. Is such a projection unique?
  2. What conditions on W and S guarantee existence?
  3. How do we compute P explicitly?

1. Uniqueness: Projection Determined by Column and Null Space ​

The first question: if a projection exists with given column and null spaces, is it unique?

Proposition

Theorem 1.1 (Uniqueness of Projection)

If two projections P and Q satisfy:

Col(P)=Col(Q)andNull(P)=Null(Q)

then P=Q.

Proof:

We prove P=Q using the interchanging property from Lecture 7.

Step 1: Show P=QP.

Since Col(P)=Col(Q), we can apply the interchanging property to Q:

Col(Q)=Null(I−Q)

Thus:

Col(P)=Null(I−Q)

This means (I−Q)P=0, so:

P=QP

Step 2: Show Q=QP.

Since Null(P)=Null(Q), we can apply the interchanging property to P:

Null(P)=Col(I−P)

Thus:

Null(Q)=Col(I−P)

This means Q(I−P)=0, so:

Q=QP

Step 3: Combine.

From Steps 1 and 2:

P=QP=Q

Therefore P=Q. □

Remark

Why This Makes Sense: Two Subspaces Determine the Projection

Recall from Lecture 7 that a projection P splits the space into two complementary parts:

  • Col(P) = vectors that are "projected onto"
  • Null(P) = vectors that are "projected away" (killed)

The interchanging property showed these two subspaces are complementary: Col(P)⊕Null(P)=Rn (every vector v has a unique decomposition v=Pv+(I−P)v).

Theorem 1.1 says: This decomposition is unique. If you specify which vectors to keep (Col(P)) and which to kill (Null(P)), there is exactly one projection doing this job.

2. Bridge Theorem: Disjoint Subspaces and Null Space Equality ​

Before constructing projections, we need a key tool from Lecture 6.

Proposition

Theorem 2.1 (Lecture 6, Theorem 3)

For matrices A and B:

Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}⟺Null(AB)=Null(B)

Remark

Why We Need This Theorem

This theorem bridges two perspectives:

  • Geometric: The subspaces Null(A) and Col(B) are disjoint (intersect only at zero)
  • Algebraic: The null space of the product AB equals the null space of B (no new dependencies created)

For projection construction, this translates a geometric condition (the desired column and null spaces must be disjoint) into an algebraic condition (AB is invertible).

We will not reprove this theorem (see Lecture 6, §1.4). Instead, we use it as a tool.

3. The Construction Problem ​

Setup: Given two subspaces W and S of Rn, we want to construct a projection P such that:

Col(P)=WandNull(P)=S

Strategy: Express the subspaces using matrices.

Example

Example 3.1: Two Subspaces in R4

Let:

W=span{(1010),(0101)}S=span{(11−1−1),(1−1−11)}

Observation:

  • dim⁡(W)=2, dim⁡(S)=2
  • dim⁡(W)+dim⁡(S)=4=n (dimensions add up to the ambient dimension)

Question: Do W and S intersect only at 0?

Check: Is there a nonzero vector in both spaces?

A vector in W has the form (abab).

A vector in S has the form c(11−1−1)+d(1−1−11)=(c+dc−d−c−d−c+d).

For these to be equal:

{a=c+db=c−da=−c−db=−c+d

From equations 1 and 3: c+d=−c−d, so c+d=0.

From equations 2 and 4: c−d=−c+d, so c−d=0.

Thus c=d=0, giving a=b=0.

Therefore W∩S={0} ✓

Key observation: For a projection to exist with Col(P)=W and Null(P)=S, we need:

  1. W∩S={0} (disjoint)
  2. dim⁡(W)+dim⁡(S)=n (complementary)

These conditions ensure every vector v∈Rn has a unique decomposition v=w+s with w∈W and s∈S.

4. Matrix Representation and the AB Invertible Condition ​

Step 1: Represent the subspaces using matrices.

  • Column space (constructive form): Write W=Col(B), where B is an n×r matrix with columns forming a basis of W. Since the columns are linearly independent, B has full column rank (rank r=dim⁡(W)).

  • Null space (descriptive form): Write S=Null(A), where A is a k×n matrix whose rows give the equations defining S. Since the equations are linearly independent, A has full row rank.

From the complementary condition dim⁡(W)+dim⁡(S)=n, we have dim⁡(S)=n−r. Since S=Null(A):

dim⁡(Null(A))=n−rank(A)=n−r

Therefore rank(A)=r. If A has full row rank, then A must be r×n.

Summary:

  • B is n×r with full column rank (rank r)
  • A is r×n with full row rank (rank r)
  • AB is r×r (square matrix)

Example

Example 3.1 (Continued): Matrix Representation

For W=span{(1010),(0101)}, we take:

B=(10011001)

For S=span{(11−1−1),(1−1−11)}, we need to find A such that Null(A)=S.

Observation: Check if W and S are orthogonal complements.

Compute inner products:

  • (1010)⋅(11−1−1)=1+0−1+0=0 ✓
  • (1010)⋅(1−1−11)=1+0−1+0=0 ✓
  • (0101)⋅(11−1−1)=0+1+0−1=0 ✓
  • (0101)⋅(1−1−11)=0−1+0+1=0 ✓

Therefore S=W⊥. The rows of A should be the basis vectors of WT:

A=(10100101)

Verify:

A(11−1−1)=(1+0−1+00+1+0−1)=(00)$$✓$$A(1−1−11)=(1+0−1+00−1+0+1)=(00)$$✓Therefore$Null(A)⊇S$.Since$A$is$2×4$withrank$2$,wehave$dim⁡(Null(A))=4−2=2=dim⁡(S)$,so$Null(A)=S$✓

Proposition

Theorem 4.1 (Matrix Representation)

Suppose W and S are subspaces of Rn with:

  • dim⁡(W)+dim⁡(S)=n
  • W∩S={0}

Then we can write:

  • W=Col(B) where B is n×r with full column rank (rank r=dim⁡(W))
  • S=Null(A) where A is r×n with full row rank (rank r)

The product AB is an r×r square matrix.

Proposition

Theorem 4.2 (AB Invertible Condition)

With notation as in Theorem 4.1, the following are equivalent:

  1. W∩S={0}
  2. Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}
  3. Null(AB)=Null(B)={0}
  4. AB is invertible

Proof:

The equivalence (1)⇔(2) is immediate from W=Col(B) and S=Null(A).

The equivalence (2)⇔(3) follows from Theorem 2.1 (the bridge theorem from Lecture 6):

Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}⟺Null(AB)=Null(B)

Since B has full column rank, Null(B)={0}. Thus:

Null(AB)=Null(B)={0}

The equivalence (3)⇔(4): Since AB is r×r (square), by Proposition 3.4 (Lecture 8), AB is invertible if and only if rank(AB)=r. By the rank-nullity theorem (Lecture 5, Theorem 4.1), rank(AB)=r if and only if Null(AB)={0}. □

Remark

Interpretation: Geometric Condition Becomes Algebraic

The geometric condition "W and S are disjoint" translates precisely to the algebraic condition "AB is invertible."

This is the power of the bridge theorem: it converts a question about subspace intersection into a question about matrix invertibility, which we can compute!

5. Construction Formula ​

Now we can state the main result.

Proposition

Theorem 5.1 (Construction of Projection)

Suppose:

  • W=Col(B) where B is n×r with full column rank
  • S=Null(A) where A is r×n with full row rank
  • W∩S={0} (equivalently, AB is invertible)

Then the unique projection P with Col(P)=W and Null(P)=S is:

P=B(AB)−1A

Proof:

We must verify three things:

  1. P2=P (projection property)
  2. Col(P)=Col(B)=W
  3. Null(P)=Null(A)=S

Verification 1: P2=P.

\begin{align*} P^2 &= \left(B(AB)^{-1}A\right) \left(B(AB)^{-1}A\right) \ &= B(AB)^{-1} (AB) (AB)^{-1} A \ &= B(AB)^{-1} A \ &= P \quad ✓ \end

Verification 2: Col(P)=Col(B).

Since P=B(AB)−1A:

Col(P)=Col(B(AB)−1A)⊆Col(B)

(Every column of P is a linear combination of columns of B.)

To show the reverse inclusion, note that:

PB=B(AB)−1A⋅B=B(AB)−1(AB)=B

Thus every column of B is in Col(P), so Col(B)⊆Col(P).

Therefore Col(P)=Col(B) ✓

Verification 3: Null(P)=Null(A).

Direction 1: Null(A)⊆Null(P).

If x∈Null(A), then Ax=0. Thus:

Px=B(AB)−1Ax=B(AB)−10=0

So x∈Null(P).

Direction 2: Null(P)⊆Null(A).

If x∈Null(P), then Px=0:

B(AB)−1Ax=0

Since B has full column rank, Null(B)={0}. Thus:

(AB)−1Ax=0

Multiplying both sides by AB:

Ax=0

So x∈Null(A).

Therefore Null(P)=Null(A) ✓

Conclusion: P=B(AB)−1A is a projection with the desired column and null spaces. By Theorem 1.1 (uniqueness), it is the unique such projection. □

Remark

Understanding the Formula

The formula P=B(AB)−1A has a beautiful structure:

  • A on the right: "Test" whether the input is in Null(A)=S
  • (AB)−1 in the middle: Rescaling factor (needed to make P2=P)
  • B on the left: Output vectors in Col(B)=W

The formula simultaneously projects onto W and along S (kills vectors in S).

6. Examples ​

Example

Example 6.1: Projection in R3

Let:

W=span{(101)},S=span{(10−1),(010)}

Step 1: Check dimensions.

  • dim⁡(W)=1, dim⁡(S)=2
  • dim⁡(W)+dim⁡(S)=3 ✓

Step 2: Check disjointness.

A vector in W has the form (a0a).

A vector in S has the form b(10−1)+c(010)=(bc−b).

For these to be equal: a=b, 0=c, a=−b. Thus a=−a, so a=0.

Therefore W∩S={0} ✓

Step 3: Construct B and A.

B=(101)(column basis for W)

For S=Null(A), we need A to be 1×3 (since r=dim⁡(W)=1) with Null(A)=S.

The vectors in S satisfy x1+x3=0 (check: (1,0,−1) gives 1+(−1)=0 ✓, (0,1,0) gives 0+0=0 ✓).

Thus:

A=(101)

Step 4: Compute AB.

AB=(101)(101)=2

Since AB=2≠0, it is invertible with (AB)−1=12.

Step 5: Compute P=B(AB)−1A.

P=(101)⋅12⋅(101)=12(101000101)

Step 6: Verify.

P2=14(101000101)(101000101)=14(202000202)=12(101000101)=P✓Col(P)=span{(101)}=W✓

Check Null(P):

P(10−1)=12(101000101)(10−1)=12(000)=0✓P(010)=12(101000101)(010)=12(000)=0✓

Therefore Null(P)⊇S. Since dim⁡(Null(P))=3−rank(P)=3−1=2=dim⁡(S), we have Null(P)=S ✓

Example

Example 6.2: Identity as Projection

Take W=Rn and S={0}.

Then:

B=In(columns form basis of Rn)A=In(Null(In)={0})AB=In⋅In=InP=In(In)−1In=In

Indeed, the identity is the projection onto Rn along {0} ✓

7. Summary ​

TIP

What We Learned

1. Uniqueness (§1):

  • A projection is uniquely determined by its column space and null space
  • If Col(P)=Col(Q) and Null(P)=Null(Q), then P=Q

2. Bridge Theorem (§2):

  • Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}⟺Null(AB)=Null(B)
  • Connects geometric disjointness to algebraic properties

3. Construction Formula (§3–5):

  • Given W=Col(B) and S=Null(A) with W∩S={0}
  • The projection P with Col(P)=W and Null(P)=S is:
P=B(AB)−1A
  • The condition W∩S={0} is equivalent to AB being invertible

4. Verification (§5):

  • P2=P follows from (AB)(AB)−1=I
  • Col(P)=Col(B) follows from PB=B
  • Null(P)=Null(A) follows from the full-rank properties of A and B

Key Insight: The construction formula P=B(AB)−1A provides an explicit, computable way to construct any projection from its desired column and null spaces.

Exercises ​

Problem

Exercise 9.1

Find the projection P onto W along S where:

W=span{(110)},S=span{(1−10),(001)}

Verify that P2=P, Col(P)=W, and Null(P)=S.

Problem

Exercise 9.2

Let P be the projection onto the xy-plane in R3. (a) What are Col(P) and Null(P)? (b) Find matrices B and A such that Col(B)=Col(P) and Null(A)=Null(P). (c) Use the construction formula to compute P.

Problem

Exercise 9.3

Suppose B is n×r with full column rank, and A is r×n with full row rank. (a) Show that if AB=Ir, then P=BA is a projection. (b) What are Col(P) and Null(P)? (c) How does this relate to Theorem 5.1?

Hint: This appeared in Lecture 8. Compare with the construction formula.

Problem

Exercise 9.4

True or false: If W and S are subspaces with dim⁡(W)+dim⁡(S)=n, then there always exists a projection P with Col(P)=W and Null(P)=S.

Justify your answer. If false, give a counterexample. If true, prove it.
